{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōgo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oogo Jinja.JPG|280px]]
Front view of the worship hall |location = {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 |coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|25|18.2|N|139|09|33.3|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōgo Shrine}} |Deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}
{{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} |ShrineRank = Former Gōsha (village shrine) |Establishment = Unknown |Alternatenames = Kondo Daimyōjin |Festivals = Daidai Kagura |Map = {{location map|Japan Gunma#Japan|label=Ōgo Shrine|lat_deg=36|lat_min=25|lat_sec=18.2|lon_deg=139|lon_min=09|lon_sec=33.3|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Ōgo Shrine''' (大胡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}}, {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}}. It was formerly classified as a Gōsha under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Historically, it served as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Ōgo Castle|ja|大胡城}} and was referred to as Kondo Shrine until the {{ill|Meiji Period|ja|明治}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been established through a partition from the {{ill|Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine|ja|三夜沢赤城神社}}, but the exact year of its foundation is unknown. During the deity's procession between {{ill|Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|ja|二宮赤城神社}} and Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Ōgo Shrine served as a resting point for the deity{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. In the {{ill|Tenshō Period|ja|天正}} (1573–1593), {{ill|Ōgo Takashige|ja|大胡高繁}}, the lord of Ōgo Castle, worshiped at the shrine. In 1589, Takashige issued a letter to the shrine priest of Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Nara Hara Kii-no-Kami, requesting a priest to perform the rituals for Kondo Shrine, which solidified the Nara family's role in maintaining the shrine{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. The shrine was renamed Ōgo Shrine in 1909. That same year, multiple smaller shrines in the vicinity, such as Sugawara Shrine and Yasaka Shrines, were merged into Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. In 1943, the aqqroach to the shrine was reoriented from the east to face south{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. Ōgo Shrine was designated as a Gōsha on April 26, 1944{{Cite web |title=Digital National Diet Library Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2961687/1/3 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. == Structure and Features == The shrine features a {{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}} (dance stage) to the right of the worship hall, where kagura performances are offered annually. A monument dedicated to Okada Yoshimasa, the first popularly elected mayor of Ōgo and a distinguished Gunma Prefectural Assembly member, stands on the premises{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=433-435}}. == Deities Enshrined == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} * 43 additional deities * Eight auxiliary deities{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}} == Festivals == === Daidai Kagura (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Maebashi City) === This ritual, performed on April 10, involves thirteen dances offered to pray for bountiful harvests. It is managed by the Daidai Kagura Preservation Society in the Ashigaru Town District{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/bunka_sports_kanko/1/3/22442.html|title=Traditional Arts in Ōgo Area|publisher=Maebashi City|accessdate=2020-05-31}}. === Ōgo Gion Festival === Held on the last Saturday and Sunday of July, this festival originates from Yasaka Shrine, although the shrine receives its sacred wands from Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1032-1234}}. == Cultural Properties == === Designated Important Cultural Properties of Maebashi City === * '''Ōgo Shrine’s Mathematical Tablet (算額)''' - Donated in 1915 by Ohara Fukutarō, a student of shipwright Matake. This rare example of Meiji-period mathematics was designated on April 5, 1994{{Cite web |title=City-Designated Cultural Properties{{!}}Maebashi City |url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/soshiki/kyoiku/bunkazaihogo/gyomu/3/2/5106.html |website=www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. * '''Daidai Kagura''' - Designated as an intangible cultural property in 1969. * '''Muku Tree (無患子)''' - Estimated to be over 300 years old, it was designated in 2008{{Cite book|和書 |title=Yearly Cultural Reports |date=2008-12-12 |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/16128}}. == location == * {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 == Access == * About 20 minutes from {{ill|Koma Interchange|ja|駒形インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kitakantō Expressway|ja|北関東自動車道}} * 25-minute walk from {{ill|Ōgo Station|ja|大胡駅}} on the Jōmō Electric Railway. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Ōgo Castle ruins|ja|大胡城址}} * {{ill|Gumma Prefectural Route 16|ja|群馬県道16号}} * {{ill|Arato River|ja|荒砥川}} * {{ill|Yōrin-ji Temple (Maebashi)|ja|養林寺}} == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=Ōgo Town History |date=1976-6-15 |year=1976 |publisher=Ōgo Town Hall |ref=harv |editor=Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/99955}} == [[ja:大胡神社]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōgo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oogo Jinja.JPG|280px]]
Front view of the worship hall |location = {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 |coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|25|18.2|N|139|09|33.3|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōgo Shrine}} |Deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}
{{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} |ShrineRank = Former Gōsha (village shrine) |Establishment = Unknown |Alternatenames = Kondo Daimyōjin |Festivals = Daidai Kagura |Map = {{location map|Japan Gunma#Japan|label=Ōgo Shrine|lat_deg=36|lat_min=25|lat_sec=18.2|lon_deg=139|lon_min=09|lon_sec=33.3|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Ōgo Shrine''' (大胡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}}, {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}}. It was formerly classified as a Gōsha under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Historically, it served as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Ōgo Castle|ja|大胡城}} and was referred to as Kondo Shrine until the {{ill|Meiji Period|ja|明治}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been established through a partition from the {{ill|Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine|ja|三夜沢赤城神社}}, but the exact year of its foundation is unknown. During the deity's procession between {{ill|Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|ja|二宮赤城神社}} and Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Ōgo Shrine served as a resting point for the deity{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. In the {{ill|Tenshō Period|ja|天正}} (1573–1593), {{ill|Ōgo Takashige|ja|大胡高繁}}, the lord of Ōgo Castle, worshiped at the shrine. In 1589, Takashige issued a letter to the shrine priest of Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Nara Hara Kii-no-Kami, requesting a priest to perform the rituals for Kondo Shrine, which solidified the Nara family's role in maintaining the shrine{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. The shrine was renamed Ōgo Shrine in 1909. That same year, multiple smaller shrines in the vicinity, such as Sugawara Shrine and Yasaka Shrines, were merged into Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. In 1943, the aqqroach to the shrine was reoriented from the east to face south{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. Ōgo Shrine was designated as a Gōsha on April 26, 1944{{Cite web |title=Digital National Diet Library Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2961687/1/3 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. == Structure and Features == The shrine features a {{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}} (dance stage) to the right of the worship hall, where kagura performances are offered annually. A monument dedicated to Okada Yoshimasa, the first popularly elected mayor of Ōgo and a distinguished Gunma Prefectural Assembly member, stands on the premises{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=433-435}}. == Deities Enshrined == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} * 43 additional deities * Eight auxiliary deities{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}} == Festivals == === Daidai Kagura (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Maebashi City) === This ritual, performed on April 10, involves thirteen dances offered to pray for bountiful harvests. It is managed by the Daidai Kagura Preservation Society in the Ashigaru Town District{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/bunka_sports_kanko/1/3/22442.html|title=Traditional Arts in Ōgo Area|publisher=Maebashi City|accessdate=2020-05-31}}. === Ōgo Gion Festival === Held on the last Saturday and Sunday of July, this festival originates from Yasaka Shrine, although the shrine receives its sacred wands from Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1032-1234}}. == Cultural Properties == === Designated Important Cultural Properties of Maebashi City === * '''Ōgo Shrine’s Mathematical Tablet (算額)''' - Donated in 1915 by Ohara Fukutarō, a student of shipwright Matake. This rare example of Meiji-period mathematics was designated on April 5, 1994{{Cite web |title=City-Designated Cultural Properties{{!}}Maebashi City |url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/soshiki/kyoiku/bunkazaihogo/gyomu/3/2/5106.html |website=www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. * '''Daidai Kagura''' - Designated as an intangible cultural property in 1969. * '''Muku Tree (無患子)''' - Estimated to be over 300 years old, it was designated in 2008{{Cite book|和書 |title=Yearly Cultural Reports |date=2008-12-12 |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/16128}}. == location == * {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 == Access == * About 20 minutes from {{ill|Koma Interchange|ja|駒形インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kitakantō Expressway|ja|北関東自動車道}} * 25-minute walk from {{ill|Ōgo Station|ja|大胡駅}} on the Jōmō Electric Railway. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Ōgo Castle ruins|ja|大胡城址}} * {{ill|Gumma Prefectural Route 16|ja|群馬県道16号}} * {{ill|Arato River|ja|荒砥川}} * {{ill|Yōrin-ji Temple (Maebashi)|ja|養林寺}} == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=Ōgo Town History |date=1976-6-15 |year=1976 |publisher=Ōgo Town Hall |ref=harv |editor=Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/99955}} == Ōgo Shrine was designated as a Gōsha on April 26, 1944{{Cite web |title=Digital National Diet Library Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2961687/1/3 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. == Structure and Features == The shrine features a {{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}} (dance stage) to the right of the worship hall, where kagura performances are offered annually. A monument dedicated to Okada Yoshimasa, the first popularly elected mayor of Ōgo and a distinguished Gunma Prefectural Assembly member, stands on the premises{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=433-435}}. == Deities Enshrined == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} * 43 additional deities * Eight auxiliary deities{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}} == Festivals == === Daidai Kagura (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Maebashi City) === This ritual, performed on April 10, involves thirteen dances offered to pray for bountiful harvests. It is managed by the Daidai Kagura Preservation Society in the Ashigaru Town District{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/bunka_sports_kanko/1/3/22442.html|title=Traditional Arts in Ōgo Area|publisher=Maebashi City|accessdate=2020-05-31}}. === Ōgo Gion Festival === Held on the last Saturday and Sunday of July, this festival originates from Yasaka Shrine, although the shrine receives its sacred wands from Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1032-1234}}. == Cultural Properties == === Designated Important Cultural Properties of Maebashi City === * '''Ōgo Shrine’s Mathematical Tablet (算額)''' - Donated in 1915 by Ohara Fukutarō, a student of shipwright Matake. This rare example of Meiji-period mathematics was designated on April 5, 1994{{Cite web |title=City-Designated Cultural Properties{{!}}Maebashi City |url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/soshiki/kyoiku/bunkazaihogo/gyomu/3/2/5106.html |website=www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. * '''Daidai Kagura''' - Designated as an intangible cultural property in 1969. * '''Muku Tree (無患子)''' - Estimated to be over 300 years old, it was designated in 2008{{Cite book|和書 |title=Yearly Cultural Reports |date=2008-12-12 |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/16128}}. == location == * {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 == Access == * About 20 minutes from {{ill|Koma Interchange|ja|駒形インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kitakantō Expressway|ja|北関東自動車道}} * 25-minute walk from {{ill|Ōgo Station|ja|大胡駅}} on the Jōmō Electric Railway. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Ōgo Castle ruins|ja|大胡城址}} * {{ill|Gumma Prefectural Route 16|ja|群馬県道16号}} * {{ill|Arato River|ja|荒砥川}} * {{ill|Yōrin-ji Temple (Maebashi)|ja|養林寺}} == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōgo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oogo Jinja.JPG|280px]]
Front view of the worship hall |location = {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 |coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|25|18.2|N|139|09|33.3|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōgo Shrine}} |Deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}
{{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} |ShrineRank = Former Gōsha (village shrine) |Establishment = Unknown |Alternatenames = Kondo Daimyōjin |Festivals = Daidai Kagura |Map = {{location map|Japan Gunma#Japan|label=Ōgo Shrine|lat_deg=36|lat_min=25|lat_sec=18.2|lon_deg=139|lon_min=09|lon_sec=33.3|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Ōgo Shrine''' (大胡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}}, {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}}. It was formerly classified as a Gōsha under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Historically, it served as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Ōgo Castle|ja|大胡城}} and was referred to as Kondo Shrine until the {{ill|Meiji Period|ja|明治}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been established through a partition from the {{ill|Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine|ja|三夜沢赤城神社}}, but the exact year of its foundation is unknown. During the deity's procession between {{ill|Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|ja|二宮赤城神社}} and Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Ōgo Shrine served as a resting point for the deity{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. In the {{ill|Tenshō Period|ja|天正}} (1573–1593), {{ill|Ōgo Takashige|ja|大胡高繁}}, the lord of Ōgo Castle, worshiped at the shrine. In 1589, Takashige issued a letter to the shrine priest of Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Nara Hara Kii-no-Kami, requesting a priest to perform the rituals for Kondo Shrine, which solidified the Nara family's role in maintaining the shrine{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. The shrine was renamed Ōgo Shrine in 1909. That same year, multiple smaller shrines in the vicinity, such as Sugawara Shrine and Yasaka Shrines, were merged into Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. In 1943, the aqqroach to the shrine was reoriented from the east to face south{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. Ōgo Shrine was designated as a Gōsha on April 26, 1944{{Cite web |title=Digital National Diet Library Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2961687/1/3 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. == Structure and Features == The shrine features a {{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}} (dance stage) to the right of the worship hall, where kagura performances are offered annually. A monument dedicated to Okada Yoshimasa, the first popularly elected mayor of Ōgo and a distinguished Gunma Prefectural Assembly member, stands on the premises{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=433-435}}. == Deities Enshrined == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} * 43 additional deities * Eight auxiliary deities{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}} == Festivals == === Daidai Kagura (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Maebashi City) === This ritual, performed on April 10, involves thirteen dances offered to pray for bountiful harvests. It is managed by the Daidai Kagura Preservation Society in the Ashigaru Town District{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/bunka_sports_kanko/1/3/22442.html|title=Traditional Arts in Ōgo Area|publisher=Maebashi City|accessdate=2020-05-31}}. === Ōgo Gion Festival === Held on the last Saturday and Sunday of July, this festival originates from Yasaka Shrine, although the shrine receives its sacred wands from Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1032-1234}}. == Cultural Properties == === Designated Important Cultural Properties of Maebashi City === * '''Ōgo Shrine’s Mathematical Tablet (算額)''' - Donated in 1915 by Ohara Fukutarō, a student of shipwright Matake. This rare example of Meiji-period mathematics was designated on April 5, 1994{{Cite web |title=City-Designated Cultural Properties{{!}}Maebashi City |url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/soshiki/kyoiku/bunkazaihogo/gyomu/3/2/5106.html |website=www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. * '''Daidai Kagura''' - Designated as an intangible cultural property in 1969. * '''Muku Tree (無患子)''' - Estimated to be over 300 years old, it was designated in 2008{{Cite book|和書 |title=Yearly Cultural Reports |date=2008-12-12 |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/16128}}. == location == * {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 == Access == * About 20 minutes from {{ill|Koma Interchange|ja|駒形インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kitakantō Expressway|ja|北関東自動車道}} * 25-minute walk from {{ill|Ōgo Station|ja|大胡駅}} on the Jōmō Electric Railway. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Ōgo Castle ruins|ja|大胡城址}} * {{ill|Gumma Prefectural Route 16|ja|群馬県道16号}} * {{ill|Arato River|ja|荒砥川}} * {{ill|Yōrin-ji Temple (Maebashi)|ja|養林寺}} == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=Ōgo Town History |date=1976-6-15 |year=1976 |publisher=Ōgo Town Hall |ref=harv |editor=Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/99955}} == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōgo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oogo Jinja.JPG|280px]]
Front view of the worship hall |location = {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 |coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|25|18.2|N|139|09|33.3|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōgo Shrine}} |Deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}
{{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} |ShrineRank = Former Gōsha (village shrine) |Establishment = Unknown |Alternatenames = Kondo Daimyōjin |Festivals = Daidai Kagura |Map = {{location map|Japan Gunma#Japan|label=Ōgo Shrine|lat_deg=36|lat_min=25|lat_sec=18.2|lon_deg=139|lon_min=09|lon_sec=33.3|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Ōgo Shrine''' (大胡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}}, {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}}. It was formerly classified as a Gōsha under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Historically, it served as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Ōgo Castle|ja|大胡城}} and was referred to as Kondo Shrine until the {{ill|Meiji Period|ja|明治}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been established through a partition from the {{ill|Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine|ja|三夜沢赤城神社}}, but the exact year of its foundation is unknown. During the deity's procession between {{ill|Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|ja|二宮赤城神社}} and Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Ōgo Shrine served as a resting point for the deity{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. In the {{ill|Tenshō Period|ja|天正}} (1573–1593), {{ill|Ōgo Takashige|ja|大胡高繁}}, the lord of Ōgo Castle, worshiped at the shrine. In 1589, Takashige issued a letter to the shrine priest of Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Nara Hara Kii-no-Kami, requesting a priest to perform the rituals for Kondo Shrine, which solidified the Nara family's role in maintaining the shrine{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. The shrine was renamed Ōgo Shrine in 1909. That same year, multiple smaller shrines in the vicinity, such as Sugawara Shrine and Yasaka Shrines, were merged into Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. In 1943, the aqqroach to the shrine was reoriented from the east to face south{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. Ōgo Shrine was designated as a Gōsha on April 26, 1944{{Cite web |title=Digital National Diet Library Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2961687/1/3 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. == Structure and Features == The shrine features a {{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}} (dance stage) to the right of the worship hall, where kagura performances are offered annually. A monument dedicated to Okada Yoshimasa, the first popularly elected mayor of Ōgo and a distinguished Gunma Prefectural Assembly member, stands on the premises{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=433-435}}. == Deities Enshrined == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} * 43 additional deities * Eight auxiliary deities{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}} == Festivals == === Daidai Kagura (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Maebashi City) === This ritual, performed on April 10, involves thirteen dances offered to pray for bountiful harvests. It is managed by the Daidai Kagura Preservation Society in the Ashigaru Town District{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/bunka_sports_kanko/1/3/22442.html|title=Traditional Arts in Ōgo Area|publisher=Maebashi City|accessdate=2020-05-31}}. === Ōgo Gion Festival === Held on the last Saturday and Sunday of July, this festival originates from Yasaka Shrine, although the shrine receives its sacred wands from Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1032-1234}}. == Cultural Properties == === Designated Important Cultural Properties of Maebashi City === * '''Ōgo Shrine’s Mathematical Tablet (算額)''' - Donated in 1915 by Ohara Fukutarō, a student of shipwright Matake. This rare example of Meiji-period mathematics was designated on April 5, 1994{{Cite web |title=City-Designated Cultural Properties{{!}}Maebashi City |url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/soshiki/kyoiku/bunkazaihogo/gyomu/3/2/5106.html |website=www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. * '''Daidai Kagura''' - Designated as an intangible cultural property in 1969. * '''Muku Tree (無患子)''' - Estimated to be over 300 years old, it was designated in 2008{{Cite book|和書 |title=Yearly Cultural Reports |date=2008-12-12 |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/16128}}. == location == * {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 == Access == * About 20 minutes from {{ill|Koma Interchange|ja|駒形インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kitakantō Expressway|ja|北関東自動車道}} * 25-minute walk from {{ill|Ōgo Station|ja|大胡駅}} on the Jōmō Electric Railway. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Ōgo Castle ruins|ja|大胡城址}} * {{ill|Gumma Prefectural Route 16|ja|群馬県道16号}} * {{ill|Arato River|ja|荒砥川}} * {{ill|Yōrin-ji Temple (Maebashi)|ja|養林寺}} == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=Ōgo Town History |date=1976-6-15 |year=1976 |publisher=Ōgo Town Hall |ref=harv |editor=Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/99955}} == revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōgo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oogo Jinja.JPG|280px]]
Front view of the worship hall |location = {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 |coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|25|18.2|N|139|09|33.3|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōgo Shrine}} |Deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}
{{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} |ShrineRank = Former Gōsha (village shrine) |Establishment = Unknown |Alternatenames = Kondo Daimyōjin |Festivals = Daidai Kagura |Map = {{location map|Japan Gunma#Japan|label=Ōgo Shrine|lat_deg=36|lat_min=25|lat_sec=18.2|lon_deg=139|lon_min=09|lon_sec=33.3|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Ōgo Shrine''' (大胡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}}, {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}}. It was formerly classified as a Gōsha under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Historically, it served as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Ōgo Castle|ja|大胡城}} and was referred to as Kondo Shrine until the {{ill|Meiji Period|ja|明治}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been established through a partition from the {{ill|Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine|ja|三夜沢赤城神社}}, but the exact year of its foundation is unknown. During the deity's procession between {{ill|Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|ja|二宮赤城神社}} and Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Ōgo Shrine served as a resting point for the deity{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. In the {{ill|Tenshō Period|ja|天正}} (1573–1593), {{ill|Ōgo Takashige|ja|大胡高繁}}, the lord of Ōgo Castle, worshiped at the shrine. In 1589, Takashige issued a letter to the shrine priest of Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Nara Hara Kii-no-Kami, requesting a priest to perform the rituals for Kondo Shrine, which solidified the Nara family's role in maintaining the shrine{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. The shrine was renamed Ōgo Shrine in 1909. That same year, multiple smaller shrines in the vicinity, such as Sugawara Shrine and Yasaka Shrines, were merged into Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. In 1943, the aqqroach to the shrine was reoriented from the east to face south{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. Ōgo Shrine was designated as a Gōsha on April 26, 1944{{Cite web |title=Digital National Diet Library Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2961687/1/3 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. == Structure and Features == The shrine features a {{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}} (dance stage) to the right of the worship hall, where kagura performances are offered annually. A monument dedicated to Okada Yoshimasa, the first popularly elected mayor of Ōgo and a distinguished Gunma Prefectural Assembly member, stands on the premises{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=433-435}}. == Deities Enshrined == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} * 43 additional deities * Eight auxiliary deities{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}} == Festivals == === Daidai Kagura (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Maebashi City) === This ritual, performed on April 10, involves thirteen dances offered to pray for bountiful harvests. It is managed by the Daidai Kagura Preservation Society in the Ashigaru Town District{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/bunka_sports_kanko/1/3/22442.html|title=Traditional Arts in Ōgo Area|publisher=Maebashi City|accessdate=2020-05-31}}. === Ōgo Gion Festival === Held on the last Saturday and Sunday of July, this festival originates from Yasaka Shrine, although the shrine receives its sacred wands from Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1032-1234}}. == Cultural Properties == === Designated Important Cultural Properties of Maebashi City === * '''Ōgo Shrine’s Mathematical Tablet (算額)''' - Donated in 1915 by Ohara Fukutarō, a student of shipwright Matake. This rare example of Meiji-period mathematics was designated on April 5, 1994{{Cite web |title=City-Designated Cultural Properties{{!}}Maebashi City |url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/soshiki/kyoiku/bunkazaihogo/gyomu/3/2/5106.html |website=www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. * '''Daidai Kagura''' - Designated as an intangible cultural property in 1969. * '''Muku Tree (無患子)''' - Estimated to be over 300 years old, it was designated in 2008{{Cite book|和書 |title=Yearly Cultural Reports |date=2008-12-12 |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/16128}}. == location == * {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 == Access == * About 20 minutes from {{ill|Koma Interchange|ja|駒形インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kitakantō Expressway|ja|北関東自動車道}} * 25-minute walk from {{ill|Ōgo Station|ja|大胡駅}} on the Jōmō Electric Railway. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Ōgo Castle ruins|ja|大胡城址}} * {{ill|Gumma Prefectural Route 16|ja|群馬県道16号}} * {{ill|Arato River|ja|荒砥川}} * {{ill|Yōrin-ji Temple (Maebashi)|ja|養林寺}} == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=Ōgo Town History |date=1976-6-15 |year=1976 |publisher=Ōgo Town Hall |ref=harv |editor=Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/99955}} == Front view of the worship hall |location = {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 |coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|25|18.2|N|139|09|33.3|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōgo Shrine}} |Deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}
{{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} |ShrineRank = Former Gōsha (village shrine) |Establishment = Unknown |Alternatenames = Kondo Daimyōjin |Festivals = Daidai Kagura |Map = {{location map|Japan Gunma#Japan|label=Ōgo Shrine|lat_deg=36|lat_min=25|lat_sec=18.2|lon_deg=139|lon_min=09|lon_sec=33.3|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Ōgo Shrine''' (大胡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}}, {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}}. It was formerly classified as a Gōsha under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Historically, it served as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Ōgo Castle|ja|大胡城}} and was referred to as Kondo Shrine until the {{ill|Meiji Period|ja|明治}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been established through a partition from the {{ill|Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine|ja|三夜沢赤城神社}}, but the exact year of its foundation is unknown. During the deity's procession between {{ill|Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|ja|二宮赤城神社}} and Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Ōgo Shrine served as a resting point for the deity{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. In the {{ill|Tenshō Period|ja|天正}} (1573–1593), {{ill|Ōgo Takashige|ja|大胡高繁}}, the lord of Ōgo Castle, worshiped at the shrine. In 1589, Takashige issued a letter to the shrine priest of Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Nara Hara Kii-no-Kami, requesting a priest to perform the rituals for Kondo Shrine, which solidified the Nara family's role in maintaining the shrine{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. The shrine was renamed Ōgo Shrine in 1909. That same year, multiple smaller shrines in the vicinity, such as Sugawara Shrine and Yasaka Shrines, were merged into Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. In 1943, the aqqroach to the shrine was reoriented from the east to face south{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. Ōgo Shrine was designated as a Gōsha on April 26, 1944{{Cite web |title=Digital National Diet Library Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2961687/1/3 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. == Structure and Features == The shrine features a {{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}} (dance stage) to the right of the worship hall, where kagura performances are offered annually. A monument dedicated to Okada Yoshimasa, the first popularly elected mayor of Ōgo and a distinguished Gunma Prefectural Assembly member, stands on the premises{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=433-435}}. == Deities Enshrined == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} * 43 additional deities * Eight auxiliary deities{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}} == Festivals == === Daidai Kagura (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Maebashi City) === This ritual, performed on April 10, involves thirteen dances offered to pray for bountiful harvests. It is managed by the Daidai Kagura Preservation Society in the Ashigaru Town District{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/bunka_sports_kanko/1/3/22442.html|title=Traditional Arts in Ōgo Area|publisher=Maebashi City|accessdate=2020-05-31}}. === Ōgo Gion Festival === Held on the last Saturday and Sunday of July, this festival originates from Yasaka Shrine, although the shrine receives its sacred wands from Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1032-1234}}. == Cultural Properties == === Designated Important Cultural Properties of Maebashi City === * '''Ōgo Shrine’s Mathematical Tablet (算額)''' - Donated in 1915 by Ohara Fukutarō, a student of shipwright Matake. This rare example of Meiji-period mathematics was designated on April 5, 1994{{Cite web |title=City-Designated Cultural Properties{{!}}Maebashi City |url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/soshiki/kyoiku/bunkazaihogo/gyomu/3/2/5106.html |website=www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. * '''Daidai Kagura''' - Designated as an intangible cultural property in 1969. * '''Muku Tree (無患子)''' - Estimated to be over 300 years old, it was designated in 2008{{Cite book|和書 |title=Yearly Cultural Reports |date=2008-12-12 |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/16128}}. == location == * {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 == Access == * About 20 minutes from {{ill|Koma Interchange|ja|駒形インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kitakantō Expressway|ja|北関東自動車道}} * 25-minute walk from {{ill|Ōgo Station|ja|大胡駅}} on the Jōmō Electric Railway. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Ōgo Castle ruins|ja|大胡城址}} * {{ill|Gumma Prefectural Route 16|ja|群馬県道16号}} * {{ill|Arato River|ja|荒砥川}} * {{ill|Yōrin-ji Temple (Maebashi)|ja|養林寺}} == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=Ōgo Town History |date=1976-6-15 |year=1976 |publisher=Ōgo Town Hall |ref=harv |editor=Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/99955}} == It was formerly classified as a Gōsha under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Historically, it served as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Ōgo Castle|ja|大胡城}} and was referred to as Kondo Shrine until the {{ill|Meiji Period|ja|明治}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been established through a partition from the {{ill|Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine|ja|三夜沢赤城神社}}, but the exact year of its foundation is unknown. During the deity's procession between {{ill|Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|ja|二宮赤城神社}} and Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Ōgo Shrine served as a resting point for the deity{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. In the {{ill|Tenshō Period|ja|天正}} (1573–1593), {{ill|Ōgo Takashige|ja|大胡高繁}}, the lord of Ōgo Castle, worshiped at the shrine. In 1589, Takashige issued a letter to the shrine priest of Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Nara Hara Kii-no-Kami, requesting a priest to perform the rituals for Kondo Shrine, which solidified the Nara family's role in maintaining the shrine{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. The shrine was renamed Ōgo Shrine in 1909. That same year, multiple smaller shrines in the vicinity, such as Sugawara Shrine and Yasaka Shrines, were merged into Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. In 1943, the aqqroach to the shrine was reoriented from the east to face south{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. Ōgo Shrine was designated as a Gōsha on April 26, 1944{{Cite web |title=Digital National Diet Library Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2961687/1/3 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. == Structure and Features == The shrine features a {{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}} (dance stage) to the right of the worship hall, where kagura performances are offered annually. A monument dedicated to Okada Yoshimasa, the first popularly elected mayor of Ōgo and a distinguished Gunma Prefectural Assembly member, stands on the premises{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=433-435}}. == Deities Enshrined == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} * 43 additional deities * Eight auxiliary deities{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}} == Festivals == === Daidai Kagura (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Maebashi City) === This ritual, performed on April 10, involves thirteen dances offered to pray for bountiful harvests. It is managed by the Daidai Kagura Preservation Society in the Ashigaru Town District{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/bunka_sports_kanko/1/3/22442.html|title=Traditional Arts in Ōgo Area|publisher=Maebashi City|accessdate=2020-05-31}}. === Ōgo Gion Festival === Held on the last Saturday and Sunday of July, this festival originates from Yasaka Shrine, although the shrine receives its sacred wands from Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1032-1234}}. == Cultural Properties == === Designated Important Cultural Properties of Maebashi City === * '''Ōgo Shrine’s Mathematical Tablet (算額)''' - Donated in 1915 by Ohara Fukutarō, a student of shipwright Matake. This rare example of Meiji-period mathematics was designated on April 5, 1994{{Cite web |title=City-Designated Cultural Properties{{!}}Maebashi City |url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/soshiki/kyoiku/bunkazaihogo/gyomu/3/2/5106.html |website=www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. * '''Daidai Kagura''' - Designated as an intangible cultural property in 1969. * '''Muku Tree (無患子)''' - Estimated to be over 300 years old, it was designated in 2008{{Cite book|和書 |title=Yearly Cultural Reports |date=2008-12-12 |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/16128}}. == location == * {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 == Access == * About 20 minutes from {{ill|Koma Interchange|ja|駒形インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kitakantō Expressway|ja|北関東自動車道}} * 25-minute walk from {{ill|Ōgo Station|ja|大胡駅}} on the Jōmō Electric Railway. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Ōgo Castle ruins|ja|大胡城址}} * {{ill|Gumma Prefectural Route 16|ja|群馬県道16号}} * {{ill|Arato River|ja|荒砥川}} * {{ill|Yōrin-ji Temple (Maebashi)|ja|養林寺}} == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} |ShrineRank = Former Gōsha (village shrine) |Establishment = Unknown |Alternatenames = Kondo Daimyōjin |Festivals = Daidai Kagura |Map = {{location map|Japan Gunma#Japan|label=Ōgo Shrine|lat_deg=36|lat_min=25|lat_sec=18.2|lon_deg=139|lon_min=09|lon_sec=33.3|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Ōgo Shrine''' (大胡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}}, {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}}. It was formerly classified as a Gōsha under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Historically, it served as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Ōgo Castle|ja|大胡城}} and was referred to as Kondo Shrine until the {{ill|Meiji Period|ja|明治}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been established through a partition from the {{ill|Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine|ja|三夜沢赤城神社}}, but the exact year of its foundation is unknown. During the deity's procession between {{ill|Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|ja|二宮赤城神社}} and Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Ōgo Shrine served as a resting point for the deity{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. In the {{ill|Tenshō Period|ja|天正}} (1573–1593), {{ill|Ōgo Takashige|ja|大胡高繁}}, the lord of Ōgo Castle, worshiped at the shrine. In 1589, Takashige issued a letter to the shrine priest of Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Nara Hara Kii-no-Kami, requesting a priest to perform the rituals for Kondo Shrine, which solidified the Nara family's role in maintaining the shrine{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. The shrine was renamed Ōgo Shrine in 1909. That same year, multiple smaller shrines in the vicinity, such as Sugawara Shrine and Yasaka Shrines, were merged into Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. In 1943, the aqqroach to the shrine was reoriented from the east to face south{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. Ōgo Shrine was designated as a Gōsha on April 26, 1944{{Cite web |title=Digital National Diet Library Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2961687/1/3 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. == Structure and Features == The shrine features a {{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}} (dance stage) to the right of the worship hall, where kagura performances are offered annually. A monument dedicated to Okada Yoshimasa, the first popularly elected mayor of Ōgo and a distinguished Gunma Prefectural Assembly member, stands on the premises{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=433-435}}. == Deities Enshrined == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} * 43 additional deities * Eight auxiliary deities{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}} == Festivals == === Daidai Kagura (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Maebashi City) === This ritual, performed on April 10, involves thirteen dances offered to pray for bountiful harvests. It is managed by the Daidai Kagura Preservation Society in the Ashigaru Town District{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/bunka_sports_kanko/1/3/22442.html|title=Traditional Arts in Ōgo Area|publisher=Maebashi City|accessdate=2020-05-31}}. === Ōgo Gion Festival === Held on the last Saturday and Sunday of July, this festival originates from Yasaka Shrine, although the shrine receives its sacred wands from Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1032-1234}}. == Cultural Properties == === Designated Important Cultural Properties of Maebashi City === * '''Ōgo Shrine’s Mathematical Tablet (算額)''' - Donated in 1915 by Ohara Fukutarō, a student of shipwright Matake. This rare example of Meiji-period mathematics was designated on April 5, 1994{{Cite web |title=City-Designated Cultural Properties{{!}}Maebashi City |url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/soshiki/kyoiku/bunkazaihogo/gyomu/3/2/5106.html |website=www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. * '''Daidai Kagura''' - Designated as an intangible cultural property in 1969. * '''Muku Tree (無患子)''' - Estimated to be over 300 years old, it was designated in 2008{{Cite book|和書 |title=Yearly Cultural Reports |date=2008-12-12 |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/16128}}. == location == * {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 == Access == * About 20 minutes from {{ill|Koma Interchange|ja|駒形インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kitakantō Expressway|ja|北関東自動車道}} * 25-minute walk from {{ill|Ōgo Station|ja|大胡駅}} on the Jōmō Electric Railway. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Ōgo Castle ruins|ja|大胡城址}} * {{ill|Gumma Prefectural Route 16|ja|群馬県道16号}} * {{ill|Arato River|ja|荒砥川}} * {{ill|Yōrin-ji Temple (Maebashi)|ja|養林寺}} == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=Ōgo Town History |date=1976-6-15 |year=1976 |publisher=Ōgo Town Hall |ref=harv |editor=Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/99955}} == Front view of the worship hall |location = {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 |coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|25|18.2|N|139|09|33.3|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōgo Shrine}} |Deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}
{{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} |ShrineRank = Former Gōsha (village shrine) |Establishment = Unknown |Alternatenames = Kondo Daimyōjin |Festivals = Daidai Kagura |Map = {{location map|Japan Gunma#Japan|label=Ōgo Shrine|lat_deg=36|lat_min=25|lat_sec=18.2|lon_deg=139|lon_min=09|lon_sec=33.3|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Ōgo Shrine''' (大胡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}}, {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}}. It was formerly classified as a Gōsha under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Historically, it served as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Ōgo Castle|ja|大胡城}} and was referred to as Kondo Shrine until the {{ill|Meiji Period|ja|明治}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been established through a partition from the {{ill|Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine|ja|三夜沢赤城神社}}, but the exact year of its foundation is unknown. During the deity's procession between {{ill|Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|ja|二宮赤城神社}} and Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Ōgo Shrine served as a resting point for the deity{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. In the {{ill|Tenshō Period|ja|天正}} (1573–1593), {{ill|Ōgo Takashige|ja|大胡高繁}}, the lord of Ōgo Castle, worshiped at the shrine. In 1589, Takashige issued a letter to the shrine priest of Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Nara Hara Kii-no-Kami, requesting a priest to perform the rituals for Kondo Shrine, which solidified the Nara family's role in maintaining the shrine{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. The shrine was renamed Ōgo Shrine in 1909. That same year, multiple smaller shrines in the vicinity, such as Sugawara Shrine and Yasaka Shrines, were merged into Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. In 1943, the aqqroach to the shrine was reoriented from the east to face south{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}}. Ōgo Shrine was designated as a Gōsha on April 26, 1944{{Cite web |title=Digital National Diet Library Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2961687/1/3 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. == Structure and Features == The shrine features a {{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}} (dance stage) to the right of the worship hall, where kagura performances are offered annually. A monument dedicated to Okada Yoshimasa, the first popularly elected mayor of Ōgo and a distinguished Gunma Prefectural Assembly member, stands on the premises{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=433-435}}. == Deities Enshrined == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} * 43 additional deities * Eight auxiliary deities{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1062-1066}} == Festivals == === Daidai Kagura (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Maebashi City) === This ritual, performed on April 10, involves thirteen dances offered to pray for bountiful harvests. It is managed by the Daidai Kagura Preservation Society in the Ashigaru Town District{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/bunka_sports_kanko/1/3/22442.html|title=Traditional Arts in Ōgo Area|publisher=Maebashi City|accessdate=2020-05-31}}. === Ōgo Gion Festival === Held on the last Saturday and Sunday of July, this festival originates from Yasaka Shrine, although the shrine receives its sacred wands from Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|qq=1032-1234}}. == Cultural Properties == === Designated Important Cultural Properties of Maebashi City === * '''Ōgo Shrine’s Mathematical Tablet (算額)''' - Donated in 1915 by Ohara Fukutarō, a student of shipwright Matake. This rare example of Meiji-period mathematics was designated on April 5, 1994{{Cite web |title=City-Designated Cultural Properties{{!}}Maebashi City |url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/soshiki/kyoiku/bunkazaihogo/gyomu/3/2/5106.html |website=www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. * '''Daidai Kagura''' - Designated as an intangible cultural property in 1969. * '''Muku Tree (無患子)''' - Estimated to be over 300 years old, it was designated in 2008{{Cite book|和書 |title=Yearly Cultural Reports |date=2008-12-12 |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/16128}}. == location == * {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 == Access == * About 20 minutes from {{ill|Koma Interchange|ja|駒形インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kitakantō Expressway|ja|北関東自動車道}} * 25-minute walk from {{ill|Ōgo Station|ja|大胡駅}} on the Jōmō Electric Railway. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Ōgo Castle ruins|ja|大胡城址}} * {{ill|Gumma Prefectural Route 16|ja|群馬県道16号}} * {{ill|Arato River|ja|荒砥川}} * {{ill|Yōrin-ji Temple (Maebashi)|ja|養林寺}} == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=Ōgo Town History |date=1976-6-15 |year=1976 |publisher=Ōgo Town Hall |ref=harv |editor=Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/99955}} == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōgo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oogo Jinja.JPG|280px]]
Front view of the worship hall |location = {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 |coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|25|18.2|N|139|09|33.3|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōgo Shrine}} |Deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}
{{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} |ShrineRank = Former Gōsha (village shrine) |Establishment = Unknown |Alternatenames = Kondo Daimyōjin |Festivals = Daidai Kagura |Map = {{location map|Japan Gunma#Japan|label=Ōgo Shrine|lat_deg=36|lat_min=25|lat_sec=18.2|lon_deg=139|lon_min=09|lon_sec=33.3|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Ōgo Shrine''' (大胡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}}, {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}}. It was formerly classified as a Gōsha under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Historically, it served as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Ōgo Castle|ja|大胡城}} and was referred to as Kondo Shrine until the {{ill|Meiji Period|ja|明治}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been established through a partition from the {{ill|Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine|ja|三夜沢赤城神社}}, but the exact year of its foundation is unknown. During the deity's procession between {{ill|Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|ja|二宮赤城神社}} and Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Ōgo Shrine served as a resting point for the deity{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. In the {{ill|Tenshō Period|ja|天正}} (1573–1593), {{ill|Ōgo Takashige|ja|大胡高繁}}, the lord of Ōgo Castle, worshiped at the shrine. In 1589, Takashige issued a letter to the shrine priest of Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Nara Hara Kii-no-Kami, requesting a priest to perform the rituals for Kondo Shrine, which solidified the Nara family's role in maintaining the shrine{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. The shrine was renamed Ōgo Shrine in 1909. That same year, multiple smaller shrines in the vicinity, such as Sugawara Shrine and Yasaka Shrines, were merged into Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. In 1943, the a11roach to the shrine was reoriented from the east to face south{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. Ōgo Shrine was designated as a Gōsha on April 26, 1944{{Cite web |title=Digital National Diet Library Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2961687/1/3 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. == Structure and Features == The shrine features a {{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}} (dance stage) to the right of the worship hall, where kagura performances are offered annually. A monument dedicated to Okada Yoshimasa, the first popularly elected mayor of Ōgo and a distinguished Gunma Prefectural Assembly member, stands on the premises{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=433-435}}. == Deities Enshrined == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} * 43 additional deities * Eight auxiliary deities{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}} == Festivals == === Daidai Kagura (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Maebashi City) === This ritual, performed on April 10, involves thirteen dances offered to pray for bountiful harvests. It is managed by the Daidai Kagura Preservation Society in the Ashigaru Town District{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/bunka_sports_kanko/1/3/22442.html|title=Traditional Arts in Ōgo Area|publisher=Maebashi City|accessdate=2020-05-31}}. === Ōgo Gion Festival === Held on the last Saturday and Sunday of July, this festival originates from Yasaka Shrine, although the shrine receives its sacred wands from Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1032-1234}}. == Cultural Properties == === Designated Important Cultural Properties of Maebashi City === * '''Ōgo Shrine’s Mathematical Tablet (算額)''' - Donated in 1915 by Ohara Fukutarō, a student of shipwright Matake. This rare example of Meiji-period mathematics was designated on April 5, 1994{{Cite web |title=City-Designated Cultural Properties{{!}}Maebashi City |url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/soshiki/kyoiku/bunkazaihogo/gyomu/3/2/5106.html |website=www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. * '''Daidai Kagura''' - Designated as an intangible cultural property in 1969. * '''Muku Tree (無患子)''' - Estimated to be over 300 years old, it was designated in 2008{{Cite book|和書 |title=Yearly Cultural Reports |date=2008-12-12 |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/16128}}. == location == * {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 == Access == * About 20 minutes from {{ill|Koma Interchange|ja|駒形インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kitakantō Expressway|ja|北関東自動車道}} * 25-minute walk from {{ill|Ōgo Station|ja|大胡駅}} on the Jōmō Electric Railway. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Ōgo Castle ruins|ja|大胡城址}} * {{ill|Gumma Prefectural Route 16|ja|群馬県道16号}} * {{ill|Arato River|ja|荒砥川}} * {{ill|Yōrin-ji Temple (Maebashi)|ja|養林寺}} == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=Ōgo Town History |date=1976-6-15 |year=1976 |publisher=Ōgo Town Hall |ref=harv |editor=Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/99955}} == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōgo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oogo Jinja.JPG|280px]]
Front view of the worship hall |location = {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 |coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|25|18.2|N|139|09|33.3|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōgo Shrine}} |Deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}
{{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} |ShrineRank = Former Gōsha (village shrine) |Establishment = Unknown |Alternatenames = Kondo Daimyōjin |Festivals = Daidai Kagura |Map = {{location map|Japan Gunma#Japan|label=Ōgo Shrine|lat_deg=36|lat_min=25|lat_sec=18.2|lon_deg=139|lon_min=09|lon_sec=33.3|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Ōgo Shrine''' (大胡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}}, {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}}. It was formerly classified as a Gōsha under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Historically, it served as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Ōgo Castle|ja|大胡城}} and was referred to as Kondo Shrine until the {{ill|Meiji Period|ja|明治}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been established through a partition from the {{ill|Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine|ja|三夜沢赤城神社}}, but the exact year of its foundation is unknown. During the deity's procession between {{ill|Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|ja|二宮赤城神社}} and Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Ōgo Shrine served as a resting point for the deity{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. In the {{ill|Tenshō Period|ja|天正}} (1573–1593), {{ill|Ōgo Takashige|ja|大胡高繁}}, the lord of Ōgo Castle, worshiped at the shrine. In 1589, Takashige issued a letter to the shrine priest of Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Nara Hara Kii-no-Kami, requesting a priest to perform the rituals for Kondo Shrine, which solidified the Nara family's role in maintaining the shrine{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. The shrine was renamed Ōgo Shrine in 1909. That same year, multiple smaller shrines in the vicinity, such as Sugawara Shrine and Yasaka Shrines, were merged into Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. In 1943, the a11roach to the shrine was reoriented from the east to face south{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. Ōgo Shrine was designated as a Gōsha on April 26, 1944{{Cite web |title=Digital National Diet Library Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2961687/1/3 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. == Structure and Features == The shrine features a {{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}} (dance stage) to the right of the worship hall, where kagura performances are offered annually. A monument dedicated to Okada Yoshimasa, the first popularly elected mayor of Ōgo and a distinguished Gunma Prefectural Assembly member, stands on the premises{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=433-435}}. == Deities Enshrined == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} * 43 additional deities * Eight auxiliary deities{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}} == Festivals == === Daidai Kagura (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Maebashi City) === This ritual, performed on April 10, involves thirteen dances offered to pray for bountiful harvests. It is managed by the Daidai Kagura Preservation Society in the Ashigaru Town District{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/bunka_sports_kanko/1/3/22442.html|title=Traditional Arts in Ōgo Area|publisher=Maebashi City|accessdate=2020-05-31}}. === Ōgo Gion Festival === Held on the last Saturday and Sunday of July, this festival originates from Yasaka Shrine, although the shrine receives its sacred wands from Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1032-1234}}. == Cultural Properties == === Designated Important Cultural Properties of Maebashi City === * '''Ōgo Shrine’s Mathematical Tablet (算額)''' - Donated in 1915 by Ohara Fukutarō, a student of shipwright Matake. This rare example of Meiji-period mathematics was designated on April 5, 1994{{Cite web |title=City-Designated Cultural Properties{{!}}Maebashi City |url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/soshiki/kyoiku/bunkazaihogo/gyomu/3/2/5106.html |website=www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. * '''Daidai Kagura''' - Designated as an intangible cultural property in 1969. * '''Muku Tree (無患子)''' - Estimated to be over 300 years old, it was designated in 2008{{Cite book|和書 |title=Yearly Cultural Reports |date=2008-12-12 |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/16128}}. == location == * {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 == Access == * About 20 minutes from {{ill|Koma Interchange|ja|駒形インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kitakantō Expressway|ja|北関東自動車道}} * 25-minute walk from {{ill|Ōgo Station|ja|大胡駅}} on the Jōmō Electric Railway. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Ōgo Castle ruins|ja|大胡城址}} * {{ill|Gumma Prefectural Route 16|ja|群馬県道16号}} * {{ill|Arato River|ja|荒砥川}} * {{ill|Yōrin-ji Temple (Maebashi)|ja|養林寺}} == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=Ōgo Town History |date=1976-6-15 |year=1976 |publisher=Ōgo Town Hall |ref=harv |editor=Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/99955}} == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōgo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oogo Jinja.JPG|280px]]
Front view of the worship hall |location = {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 |coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|25|18.2|N|139|09|33.3|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōgo Shrine}} |Deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}
{{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} |ShrineRank = Former Gōsha (village shrine) |Establishment = Unknown |Alternatenames = Kondo Daimyōjin |Festivals = Daidai Kagura |Map = {{location map|Japan Gunma#Japan|label=Ōgo Shrine|lat_deg=36|lat_min=25|lat_sec=18.2|lon_deg=139|lon_min=09|lon_sec=33.3|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Ōgo Shrine''' (大胡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}}, {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}}. It was formerly classified as a Gōsha under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Historically, it served as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Ōgo Castle|ja|大胡城}} and was referred to as Kondo Shrine until the {{ill|Meiji Period|ja|明治}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been established through a partition from the {{ill|Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine|ja|三夜沢赤城神社}}, but the exact year of its foundation is unknown. During the deity's procession between {{ill|Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|ja|二宮赤城神社}} and Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Ōgo Shrine served as a resting point for the deity{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. In the {{ill|Tenshō Period|ja|天正}} (1573–1593), {{ill|Ōgo Takashige|ja|大胡高繁}}, the lord of Ōgo Castle, worshiped at the shrine. In 1589, Takashige issued a letter to the shrine priest of Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Nara Hara Kii-no-Kami, requesting a priest to perform the rituals for Kondo Shrine, which solidified the Nara family's role in maintaining the shrine{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. The shrine was renamed Ōgo Shrine in 1909. That same year, multiple smaller shrines in the vicinity, such as Sugawara Shrine and Yasaka Shrines, were merged into Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. In 1943, the a11roach to the shrine was reoriented from the east to face south{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. Ōgo Shrine was designated as a Gōsha on April 26, 1944{{Cite web |title=Digital National Diet Library Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2961687/1/3 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. == Structure and Features == The shrine features a {{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}} (dance stage) to the right of the worship hall, where kagura performances are offered annually. A monument dedicated to Okada Yoshimasa, the first popularly elected mayor of Ōgo and a distinguished Gunma Prefectural Assembly member, stands on the premises{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=433-435}}. == Deities Enshrined == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} * 43 additional deities * Eight auxiliary deities{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}} == Festivals == === Daidai Kagura (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Maebashi City) === This ritual, performed on April 10, involves thirteen dances offered to pray for bountiful harvests. It is managed by the Daidai Kagura Preservation Society in the Ashigaru Town District{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/bunka_sports_kanko/1/3/22442.html|title=Traditional Arts in Ōgo Area|publisher=Maebashi City|accessdate=2020-05-31}}. === Ōgo Gion Festival === Held on the last Saturday and Sunday of July, this festival originates from Yasaka Shrine, although the shrine receives its sacred wands from Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1032-1234}}. == Cultural Properties == === Designated Important Cultural Properties of Maebashi City === * '''Ōgo Shrine’s Mathematical Tablet (算額)''' - Donated in 1915 by Ohara Fukutarō, a student of shipwright Matake. This rare example of Meiji-period mathematics was designated on April 5, 1994{{Cite web |title=City-Designated Cultural Properties{{!}}Maebashi City |url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/soshiki/kyoiku/bunkazaihogo/gyomu/3/2/5106.html |website=www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. * '''Daidai Kagura''' - Designated as an intangible cultural property in 1969. * '''Muku Tree (無患子)''' - Estimated to be over 300 years old, it was designated in 2008{{Cite book|和書 |title=Yearly Cultural Reports |date=2008-12-12 |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/16128}}. == location == * {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 == Access == * About 20 minutes from {{ill|Koma Interchange|ja|駒形インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kitakantō Expressway|ja|北関東自動車道}} * 25-minute walk from {{ill|Ōgo Station|ja|大胡駅}} on the Jōmō Electric Railway. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Ōgo Castle ruins|ja|大胡城址}} * {{ill|Gumma Prefectural Route 16|ja|群馬県道16号}} * {{ill|Arato River|ja|荒砥川}} * {{ill|Yōrin-ji Temple (Maebashi)|ja|養林寺}} == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=Ōgo Town History |date=1976-6-15 |year=1976 |publisher=Ōgo Town Hall |ref=harv |editor=Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/99955}} == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōgo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oogo Jinja.JPG|280px]]
Front view of the worship hall |location = {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 |coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|25|18.2|N|139|09|33.3|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōgo Shrine}} |Deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}
{{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} |ShrineRank = Former Gōsha (village shrine) |Establishment = Unknown |Alternatenames = Kondo Daimyōjin |Festivals = Daidai Kagura |Map = {{location map|Japan Gunma#Japan|label=Ōgo Shrine|lat_deg=36|lat_min=25|lat_sec=18.2|lon_deg=139|lon_min=09|lon_sec=33.3|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Ōgo Shrine''' (大胡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}}, {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}}. It was formerly classified as a Gōsha under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Historically, it served as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Ōgo Castle|ja|大胡城}} and was referred to as Kondo Shrine until the {{ill|Meiji Period|ja|明治}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been established through a partition from the {{ill|Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine|ja|三夜沢赤城神社}}, but the exact year of its foundation is unknown. During the deity's procession between {{ill|Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|ja|二宮赤城神社}} and Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Ōgo Shrine served as a resting point for the deity{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. In the {{ill|Tenshō Period|ja|天正}} (1573–1593), {{ill|Ōgo Takashige|ja|大胡高繁}}, the lord of Ōgo Castle, worshiped at the shrine. In 1589, Takashige issued a letter to the shrine priest of Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Nara Hara Kii-no-Kami, requesting a priest to perform the rituals for Kondo Shrine, which solidified the Nara family's role in maintaining the shrine{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. The shrine was renamed Ōgo Shrine in 1909. That same year, multiple smaller shrines in the vicinity, such as Sugawara Shrine and Yasaka Shrines, were merged into Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. In 1943, the a11roach to the shrine was reoriented from the east to face south{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. Ōgo Shrine was designated as a Gōsha on April 26, 1944{{Cite web |title=Digital National Diet Library Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2961687/1/3 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. == Structure and Features == The shrine features a {{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}} (dance stage) to the right of the worship hall, where kagura performances are offered annually. A monument dedicated to Okada Yoshimasa, the first popularly elected mayor of Ōgo and a distinguished Gunma Prefectural Assembly member, stands on the premises{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=433-435}}. == Deities Enshrined == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} * 43 additional deities * Eight auxiliary deities{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}} == Festivals == === Daidai Kagura (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Maebashi City) === This ritual, performed on April 10, involves thirteen dances offered to pray for bountiful harvests. It is managed by the Daidai Kagura Preservation Society in the Ashigaru Town District{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/bunka_sports_kanko/1/3/22442.html|title=Traditional Arts in Ōgo Area|publisher=Maebashi City|accessdate=2020-05-31}}. === Ōgo Gion Festival === Held on the last Saturday and Sunday of July, this festival originates from Yasaka Shrine, although the shrine receives its sacred wands from Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1032-1234}}. == Cultural Properties == === Designated Important Cultural Properties of Maebashi City === * '''Ōgo Shrine’s Mathematical Tablet (算額)''' - Donated in 1915 by Ohara Fukutarō, a student of shipwright Matake. This rare example of Meiji-period mathematics was designated on April 5, 1994{{Cite web |title=City-Designated Cultural Properties{{!}}Maebashi City |url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/soshiki/kyoiku/bunkazaihogo/gyomu/3/2/5106.html |website=www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. * '''Daidai Kagura''' - Designated as an intangible cultural property in 1969. * '''Muku Tree (無患子)''' - Estimated to be over 300 years old, it was designated in 2008{{Cite book|和書 |title=Yearly Cultural Reports |date=2008-12-12 |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/16128}}. == location == * {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 == Access == * About 20 minutes from {{ill|Koma Interchange|ja|駒形インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kitakantō Expressway|ja|北関東自動車道}} * 25-minute walk from {{ill|Ōgo Station|ja|大胡駅}} on the Jōmō Electric Railway. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Ōgo Castle ruins|ja|大胡城址}} * {{ill|Gumma Prefectural Route 16|ja|群馬県道16号}} * {{ill|Arato River|ja|荒砥川}} * {{ill|Yōrin-ji Temple (Maebashi)|ja|養林寺}} == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=Ōgo Town History |date=1976-6-15 |year=1976 |publisher=Ōgo Town Hall |ref=harv |editor=Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/99955}} == |url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/soshiki/kyoiku/bunkazaihogo/gyomu/3/2/5106.html |website=www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. * '''Daidai Kagura''' - Designated as an {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōgo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Oogo Jinja.JPG|280px]]
Front view of the worship hall |location = {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 |coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|25|18.2|N|139|09|33.3|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōgo Shrine}} |Deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}
{{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} |ShrineRank = Former Gōsha (village shrine) |Establishment = Unknown |Alternatenames = Kondo Daimyōjin |Festivals = Daidai Kagura |Map = {{location map|Japan Gunma#Japan|label=Ōgo Shrine|lat_deg=36|lat_min=25|lat_sec=18.2|lon_deg=139|lon_min=09|lon_sec=33.3|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Ōgo Shrine''' (大胡神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}}, {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}}. It was formerly classified as a Gōsha under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Historically, it served as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Ōgo Castle|ja|大胡城}} and was referred to as Kondo Shrine until the {{ill|Meiji Period|ja|明治}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been established through a partition from the {{ill|Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine|ja|三夜沢赤城神社}}, but the exact year of its foundation is unknown. During the deity's procession between {{ill|Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|ja|二宮赤城神社}} and Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Ōgo Shrine served as a resting point for the deity{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. In the {{ill|Tenshō Period|ja|天正}} (1573–1593), {{ill|Ōgo Takashige|ja|大胡高繁}}, the lord of Ōgo Castle, worshiped at the shrine. In 1589, Takashige issued a letter to the shrine priest of Sanyazawa Akagi Shrine, Nara Hara Kii-no-Kami, requesting a priest to perform the rituals for Kondo Shrine, which solidified the Nara family's role in maintaining the shrine{{Sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. The shrine was renamed Ōgo Shrine in 1909. That same year, multiple smaller shrines in the vicinity, such as Sugawara Shrine and Yasaka Shrines, were merged into Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. In 1943, the a11roach to the shrine was reoriented from the east to face south{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}}. Ōgo Shrine was designated as a Gōsha on April 26, 1944{{Cite web |title=Digital National Diet Library Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2961687/1/3 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. == Structure and Features == The shrine features a {{ill|Kaguraden|ja|神楽殿}} (dance stage) to the right of the worship hall, where kagura performances are offered annually. A monument dedicated to Okada Yoshimasa, the first popularly elected mayor of Ōgo and a distinguished Gunma Prefectural Assembly member, stands on the premises{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=433-435}}. == Deities Enshrined == * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto}} * 43 additional deities * Eight auxiliary deities{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1062-1066}} == Festivals == === Daidai Kagura (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Maebashi City) === This ritual, performed on April 10, involves thirteen dances offered to pray for bountiful harvests. It is managed by the Daidai Kagura Preservation Society in the Ashigaru Town District{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/bunka_sports_kanko/1/3/22442.html|title=Traditional Arts in Ōgo Area|publisher=Maebashi City|accessdate=2020-05-31}}. === Ōgo Gion Festival === Held on the last Saturday and Sunday of July, this festival originates from Yasaka Shrine, although the shrine receives its sacred wands from Ōgo Shrine{{sfn|Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee|1976|11=1032-1234}}. == Cultural Properties == === Designated Important Cultural Properties of Maebashi City === * '''Ōgo Shrine’s Mathematical Tablet (算額)''' - Donated in 1915 by Ohara Fukutarō, a student of shipwright Matake. This rare example of Meiji-period mathematics was designated on April 5, 1994{{Cite web |title=City-Designated Cultural Properties{{!}}Maebashi City |url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/soshiki/kyoiku/bunkazaihogo/gyomu/3/2/5106.html |website=www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp |access-date=2024-08-23}}. * '''Daidai Kagura''' - Designated as an intangible cultural property in 1969. * '''Muku Tree (無患子)''' - Estimated to be over 300 years old, it was designated in 2008{{Cite book|和書 |title=Yearly Cultural Reports |date=2008-12-12 |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/16128}}. == location == * {{ill|Gumma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}} {{ill|Maebashi City|ja|前橋市}} Kawarahama Town 615 == Access == * About 20 minutes from {{ill|Koma Interchange|ja|駒形インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kitakantō Expressway|ja|北関東自動車道}} * 25-minute walk from {{ill|Ōgo Station|ja|大胡駅}} on the Jōmō Electric Railway. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Ōgo Castle ruins|ja|大胡城址}} * {{ill|Gumma Prefectural Route 16|ja|群馬県道16号}} * {{ill|Arato River|ja|荒砥川}} * {{ill|Yōrin-ji Temple (Maebashi)|ja|養林寺}} == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書 |title=Ōgo Town History |date=1976-6-15 |year=1976 |publisher=Ōgo Town Hall |ref=harv |editor=Ōgo Town History Compilation Committee |url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/99955}} == External
